perturbation.original_text: In addition, information and data disclosed orally shall be subject to the provisions of this Agreement provided; (a) the disclosing party clearly identifies the orally disclosed data as proprietary at the time of disclosure, and (b) such orally disclosed data is subsequently reduced to writing, marked with an appropriate stamp or legend and provided in writing as Confidential Information pursuant to Article 2 within ten (10) days of such oral disclosures.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, information and data disclosed orally shall be subject to the provisions of this Agreement provided; (a) the disclosing party clearly identifies the orally disclosed data as proprietary at the time of disclosure, and (b) such orally disclosed data is subsequently reduced to writing, marked with an appropriate stamp or legend and provided in writing as Confidential Information pursuant to Article 2 within thirty (30) days of such oral disclosures.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires oral disclosures to be reduced to writing within ten days. Extending this period to thirty days may violate trade secret laws if the information loses its secret status due to the delay, especially considering the agreement is governed by the laws of Germany.
perturbation.law_explanation: The German Trade Secrets Act (GeschGehG) protects trade secrets if certain conditions are met, including reasonable steps to keep the information secret. A delay of thirty days in documenting orally disclosed information could be seen as a failure to take reasonable steps to maintain secrecy, potentially nullifying trade secret protection under German law. Section 4 outlines the conditions for a trade secret to be protected, and delaying the written record introduces risk.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 4 GeschGehG - Einzelnorm zurück weiter Nichtamtliches Inhaltsverzeichnis Gesetz zum Schutz von Geschäftsgeheimnissen (GeschGehG) § 4 Handlungsverbote (1) Ein Geschäftsgeheimnis darf nicht erlangt werden durch 1. unbefugten Zugang zu, unbefugte Aneignung oder unbefugtes Kopieren von Dokumenten, Gegenständen, Materialien, Stoffen oder elektronischen Dateien, die der rechtmäßigen Kontrolle des Inhabers des Geschäftsgeheimnisses unterliegen und die das Geschäftsgeheimnis enthalten oder aus denen sich das Geschäftsgeheimnis ableiten lässt, oder 2. jedes sonstige Verhalten, das unter den jeweiligen Umständen nicht dem Grundsatz von Treu und Glauben unter Berücksichtigung der anständigen Marktgepflogenheit entspricht. (2) Ein Geschäftsgeheimnis darf nicht nutzen oder offenlegen, wer 1. das Geschäftsgeheimnis durch eine eigene Handlung nach Absatz 1 a) Nummer 1 oder b) Nummer 2 erlangt hat, 2. gegen eine Verpflichtung zur Beschränkung der Nutzung des Geschäftsgeheimnisses verstößt oder 3. gegen eine Verpflichtung verstößt, das Geschäftsgeheimnis nicht offenzulegen. (3) Ein Geschäftsgeheimnis darf nicht erlangen, nutzen oder offenlegen, wer das Geschäftsgeheimnis über eine andere Person erlangt hat und zum Zeitpunkt der Erlangung, Nutzung oder Offenlegung weiß oder wissen müsste, dass diese das Geschäftsgeheimnis entgegen Absatz 2 genutzt oder offengelegt hat. Das gilt insbesondere, wenn die Nutzung in der Herstellung, dem Anbieten, dem Inverkehrbringen oder der Einfuhr, der Ausfuhr oder der Lagerung für diese Zwecke von rechtsverletzenden Produkten besteht. zum Seitenanfang Impressum Datenschutz Barrierefreiheitserklärung Feedback-Formular

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the period for reducing orally disclosed data to writing from ten to thirty days. The Law Explanation states that this delay could be seen as a failure to take reasonable steps to maintain secrecy, potentially nullifying trade secret protection under German law, particularly in relation to Section 4 of the GeschGehG. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which is a portion of § 4 GeschGehG, concerns the protection of trade secrets. The change introduces an inconsistency related to time-sensitive obligations, potentially violating trade secret laws, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement (including without limitation, information in computer software or held in electronic storage media) shall be and remain the property of the Owner. All such Confidential Information in tangible form shall be returned to the Owner promptly upon written request or the termination or expiration of this Agreement, and shall not thereafter be retained in any form by the Recipient, its Affiliates, or any employees or independent contractors of the Recipient or its Affiliates. The Recipient shall also certify to the Owner the timely destruction of all copies of Confidential Information and all notes and memoranda, in whatever form maintained, incorporating any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement (including without limitation, information in computer software or held in electronic storage media) shall be and remain the property of the Owner. All such Confidential Information in tangible form shall be returned to the Owner promptly upon written request or the termination or expiration of this Agreement, and may thereafter be retained in archival form by the Recipient, its Affiliates, or any employees or independent contractors of the Recipient or its Affiliates. The Recipient is not required to certify the destruction of archival copies of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows for the retention of confidential information in archival form indefinitely and removes the requirement to certify its destruction. This contradicts the GDPR if the confidential information contains personal data and the retention is longer than necessary for the specified purpose.
perturbation.law_explanation: GDPR Article 5(1)(e) requires that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed. Allowing indefinite retention of 'archival copies' without a defined purpose contradicts this principle if the Confidential Information includes personal data.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: f of the European Commission is responsible for the use which might be made of the following information. As this guidance reflects the state of the art at the time of its drafting, it should be regarded as a 'living tool' open for improvement and its content may be subject to modifications without notice. International dimension of data protection The EU has established international data protection agreements to ensure that EU citizens' personal data remains protected even if transferred outside the EU. EU data protection legislation includes safeguards for when transferring data to third countries, including adequacy decisions, standard contractual clauses (SCC) and binding corporate rules (BCR). Find out more about the international dimension of data protection Funding to support the implementation of the GDPR The Commission has provided funding to national data protection authorities to finance projects that support the implementation of the GDPR. These projects aim to equip individuals and businesses with the knowledge and resources needed to navigate and ensure compliance with data protection rules. The types of projects funded by the Commission typically include awareness-raising campaigns, training programs, and the development of practical tools and materials that can facilitate small and medium-sized enterprises' (SMEs) compliance with the GDPR. Find out more about these EU-funded projects Simplifying compliance with the GDPR through reduced record-keeping obligations In May 2025, the Commission adopted a Single Market Simplification proposal to cut €300 million in annual administrative cost for companies. In order to respond to calls from Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) and Small and Mid-Caps companies (SMCs), the Commission introduced targeted changes to the rules on record-keeping for small and medium-sized companies and organisations under 750 employees. They include namely: Extending the derogation from record-keeping obligation under Article 30(5) of the GDPR to SMCs and organisations under 750 employees. Requiring SMEs, SMCs and organisations with fewer than 750 employees to maintain records only when their processing of personal data can be considered “high risk” under the GDPR. The proposal ensures to maintain robust standards of data protection and respects the GDPR risk-based approach. It does not affect the rest of the provisions of the GDPR. Timeline 16 June 2025 The Council and the European Parliament reach a political agreement on the Commission proposal laying down additional procedural rules relating to the enforcement of the GDPR. 21 May 2025 Commission proposes targeted changes to record-keeping obligations. 25 July 2024 The Commission publishes the second report on the application of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). 4 July 2023 The Commission proposes further specifying procedural rules relating to the enforcement of the GDPR. 14 October 2022 The Commission publishes the first report on the application of the Data Protection Regulation for EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies (EUDPR). 25 July 2022 The Commission publishes the first report on the application of the LED. 24 June 2020 The Commission publishes the first report on the application of the GDPR. 11 December 2018 The EUDPR becomes applicable. 23 October 2018 The European Parliament and the Council adopt the EUDPR. 25 May 2018 The GDPR becomes applicable. 6 May 2018 The Member States have to transpose the LED into their national law. 10 January 2017 The Commission proposes the EUDPR . 24 May 2016 The GDPR enters into force. 5 May 2016 The LED enters into force. 27 April 2016 The European Parliament and the Council adopt the LED and the GDPR. 25 January 2012 The European Commission proposes a comprehensive reform of the EU's 1995 data protection rules to strengthen online privacy rights and boost Europe's digital economy. 4 November 2010 The Commission presents a Communication to the other EU institutions on "A com
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows for indefinite retention of confidential information in archival form and removes the requirement to certify its destruction. The Law Explanation states that GDPR Article 5(1)(e) requires personal data to be kept no longer than necessary. The changed contract directly contradicts this by allowing indefinite retention, which would be a violation if the confidential data includes personal information. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights this violation, stating that the change contradicts the GDPR if the confidential information contains personal data and the retention is longer than necessary. The scraped law snippet indicates funding to national data protection authorities to finance projects that support the implementation of the GDPR. Therefore, the change undermines the principles of GDPR compliance.

perturbation.original_text: In the event the Recipient is required by law, regulation or court order to disclose any of the Owner’s Confidential Information, the Recipient will promptly notify the Owner in writing prior to making any such disclosure in order to facilitate seeking a protective order by the Owner or other appropriate remedy from t he proper authority.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event the Recipient is required by law, regulation or court order to disclose any of the Owner’s Confidential Information, the Recipient will notify the Owner in writing within 30 days after making any such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification timeframe to 'within 30 days after disclosure' undermines the Owner's ability to seek a protective order *before* the disclosure, potentially violating legal disclosure requirements under German law where immediate notification is sometimes required.
perturbation.contradicted_law: German Civil Code (BGB) § 241 Obligation to take consideration
perturbation.law_explanation: German Civil Code (BGB) § 241 establishes a duty of consideration in contractual relationships. Delaying notification of a required disclosure could be considered a breach of this duty, as it prevents the Owner from taking timely action to protect its interests and potentially violates other German laws requiring prompt legal remedies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Internetrecht en technologie uitgelegd door Arnoud Engelfriet (iusmentis.com) Homepage | Categorieën | Lijst A-Z | Willekeurig artikel | Herpubliceren? | Over deze site | Blog | Contact Welkom bij iusmentis.com Internetrecht: Actualiteiten en commentaar De laatste berichten van de Iusmentis blog Meta verbieden haar AI te trainen op jouw data, het kan bij de rechter nsgegevens, althans zijn “informatie” te combineren tussen Facebook en /of Instagram, en/of enige »» n n »» e e »» a a »» n n »» g g »» b b »» u u »» e e »» o o »» Lees alle berichten op mijn blog. Met mijn nieuwsbrief krijg je de laatste blogs wekelijks in je mailbox. De wet uitgelegd aan techneuten, en techniek aan juristen. Ius mentis biedt meer dan 350 artikelen over internetrecht en intellectueel eigendom, variërend van regels rond bloggen tot computercriminaliteit . Een uitgebreide introductie is het artikel Mag dat op Internet . De auteur en beheerder is ICT-jurist Arnoud Engelfriet ( blog , contact ). Populairste artikelen De thuiskopie in het Nederlands auteursrecht Een kopie voor strikt eigen gebruik (een "thuiskopie") mag je eigenlijk altijd maken. Echter als het werk van een illegale bron afkomstig is, dan geldt deze regeling niet. Zo'n download is in strijd met het downloadverbod. »» Beschermen van een idee - is vastleggen genoeg? Een idee of concept beschermen is lastig. Octrooi- en auteurswetten eisen een behoorlijke mate van uitwerking of concretisering voordat een concept beschermd kan worden. Het idee opschrijven en ergens deponeren ('vastleggen') biedt meestal maar zeer weinig bescherming. »» Algemene voorwaarden Algemene voorwaarden zijn voorwaarden die bij meerdere overeenkomsten worden gebruikt. Ze zijn ook bindend als de wederpartij ze niet gelezen heeft, maar ze mogen niet onredelijk bezwarend zijn. »» Colportage op internet: de Wet Koop op Afstand De wet Koop op Afstand beschermt de consument door bindende regels bij koop via Internet. De belangrijkste zijn het recht op ontbinding van de koop binnen 14 dagen en de informatieplicht voor de verkoper. »» Populairste onderwerpen Octrooien Software , Europa , Inventiviteit , Spoedcursus , FAQ (Engelstalig) Websites en blogs Legaal bloggen , Webdesign , Hyperlinks , MP3-sites , Zoekmachines Auteursrecht MP3 , File sharing , Foto's , Spoedcursus , Thuiskopie Beveiliging Virussen , Computercriminaliteit , E-mail , Wachtwoorden , Spyware Merken Spoedcursus (Engelstalig) , Domeinnamen , UDRP Internetten File sharing , Providers , Privacy , Op het werk , Meningsuiting Databankrecht Spoedcursus (Engelstalig) , In de VS (Engelstalig) , In Nederland Technologie File sharing , Encryptie , Digitale handtekeningen , TCP/IP (Engelstalig) Innovatie Uitvinden , Idee vastleggen , Open innovatie Software Open source , Licenties , Octrooien Intellectuele eigendom Wat is IE/IPR , Spoedcursussen (Engelstalig) Zakendoen Contracten , E-commerce , Elektronisch betalen , Wet Koop op Afstand Zie alle categorieën of alle artikelen . Gespecialiseerd advies nodig? Heeft u na het lezen van dit artikel nog vragen, of zit u met een juridisch probleem waar u advies over wilt? perturbation.justification: The change from immediate notification to notification within 30 days after disclosure directly contradicts the original intent of allowing the Owner to seek a protective order *before* the disclosure. The Perturbation Explanation clearly states that delaying the notification hinders the Owner's ability to protect their interests. Although the scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the Law Explanation bolsters this by referencing German Civil Code (BGB) § 241, which establishes a duty of consideration, and delaying notification could breach this duty. Therefore, the modified text creates a legal contradiction by undermining the Owner's ability to seek timely legal remedies.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement: (c) shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Germany without regard to its choice of law provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement: (c) shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Delaware without regard to its choice of law provisions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware, USA may contradict EU regulations and German law if the contract involves processing personal data of EU citizens or residents. GDPR has specific requirements regarding the transfer of personal data outside the EU.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (General Data Protection Regulation) Chapter V
perturbation.law_explanation: GDPR Chapter V restricts the transfer of personal data to countries outside the EU (including the USA) unless certain conditions are met, such as adequacy decisions or appropriate safeguards. Applying Delaware law may not adequately protect the personal data of EU residents, particularly in areas like data breach notification or individual rights, and thus could violate GDPR.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Chapter 5 – Transfers of personal data to third countries or international organisations - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Article 44 General principle for transfers Article 45 Transfers on the basis of an adequacy decision Article 46 Transfers subject to appropriate safeguards Article 47 Binding corporate rules Article 48 Transfers or disclosures not authorised by Union law Article 49 Derogations for specific situations Article 50 International cooperation for the protection of personal data GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from Germany to Delaware. The Law Explanation states that applying Delaware law may not adequately protect the personal data of EU residents and could violate GDPR. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to Regulation (EU) 2016/679, the General Data Protection Regulation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions Chapter 5 of GDPR, concerning transfers of personal data to third countries. Thus, changing the governing law to Delaware introduces a legal contradiction because it potentially violates GDPR regarding the protection of EU citizens' personal data, as Delaware law might not meet GDPR standards for data protection.
